blocks.title: Overview
blocks.text:
Of all the insights embedded in the DVULI curriculum, this one may be the most personally confronting: the greatest systemic problem in most ministry organizations is not structural or strategic — it is the degree to which leaders and team members have closed their hearts toward one another and are operating from a 'Heart at War' rather than a 'Heart at Peace.' This session is built on the Arbinger Institute's Anatomy of Peace framework, adapted for DVULI through the A Believer's Guide to Relating to Others with a Heart at Peace. It confronts the single most pervasive source of organizational breakdown: self-deception — the tendency to see others as objects (obstacles, vehicles, irrelevancies) rather than as image-bearers of God with their own needs, objectives, and challenges.

The session's foundational move is introducing two 'Ways of Being': the Outward Mindset (seeing others as people — full, complex, dignified human beings) and the Inward Mindset (seeing others as objects — instruments of my goals, threats to my security, irrelevancies to my mission). The Arbinger framework identifies how we go 'In the Box' — when we betray our sense of what we should do for another person (called self-betrayal), we enter a resistant, inward way of being that then justifies itself through horribilizing (making others worse than they are) and blaming (attributing our problems to others' deficiencies). The Collusion Diagram makes visible the destructive system that emerges when two people or groups are simultaneously in the box toward each other — each acting in ways that produce the very behaviors in the other that justify staying in the box.

The session's practical resolution is the SAM process — See Others (understand their needs, objectives, and challenges), Adjust Efforts (change what you are doing to be more genuinely helpful), Measure Impact (hold yourself accountable for whether your actions actually make things better for the other person) — as the three-step process for restoring a Heart at Peace and getting 'Out of the Box.' The biblical framework grounds this in Philippians 2:1–8 (self-emptying love), Galatians 6:1–5 (burden-bearing community), and the Imago Dei doctrine (every person bears the image of God and therefore has inherent dignity). This session is the personal application of the systems thinking from Session 13: the Inward Mindset is itself a mental model at the deepest level of the iceberg, producing structural patterns (avoidance, silos, passive aggression) that generate behavioral events (conflict, turnover, ministry stagnation).
blocks.html:
Session 14

Heart at War, Heart at Peace

The Outward Mindset and the Leader Who Sees People as People

Marcus had led his youth ministry for eleven years. By every external measure, the program was flourishing — attendance had grown, the budget had stabilized, and the city recognized his organization as a model for community engagement. Yet something had gone quietly wrong inside the walls. His two most gifted volunteer coordinators had stopped returning each other's emails. Staff meetings had become exercises in managed silence, where disagreements were never raised and nothing was ever truly resolved. One of his long-time leaders had quietly resigned, citing "a difficult team environment." Marcus could not put his finger on what had broken down — the structure was sound, the strategy was clear, the mission had not changed. But the organization felt hollow at its core.

What Marcus was experiencing is not rare. Across ministry organizations everywhere, leaders encounter the same baffling reality: systems that look healthy on paper but feel broken in practice. According to the Arbinger Institute (2016), the root cause is almost never strategic or structural. It is something far more intimate and far more confronting: the degree to which the people inside an organization have closed their hearts toward one another. This session names that dynamic directly — and offers a path through it.

The Hidden Source of Organizational Breakdown

DVULI's curriculum has equipped you to read systems — to look beneath observable events, through behavioral patterns and structural habits, all the way down to the mental models that quietly govern how an organization moves through the world (Arbinger Institute, 2016). In Session 13, you applied the Systems Iceberg to the ministry situations you know best. What you may not have fully anticipated is that the deepest level of that iceberg — the stratum of foundational mental models — is not primarily a set of ideas about strategy or theology. It is, most fundamentally, a way of seeing people.

The Arbinger Institute has spent decades researching what actually produces organizational dysfunction, conflict, and stagnation. Their finding is consistently sobering: the single most pervasive driver of breakdown in teams, families, and institutions is not a lack of skill, resources, or vision. It is what they call self-deception — the human tendency to see others not as full people with their own needs, objectives, and burdens, but as objects within our own story (Arbinger Institute, 2000). When this way of seeing takes root in a leader — or, worse, when it becomes the dominant posture of an entire team — no structural fix or strategic innovation can reach the actual source of the problem.

This session is built on the Arbinger framework as adapted for DVULI through A Believer's Guide to Relating to Others with a Heart at Peace — a guide that grounds the Arbinger principles in Scripture and applies them to the distinctive challenges of Christian ministry leadership (RDV Corporation, 2024). What you will encounter here is not merely organizational psychology. It is a confrontation with the way we habitually use, dismiss, and misrepresent the people God has placed alongside us — and a rigorously practical path back toward what Paul called "the same mind that was in Christ Jesus" (Philippians 2:5, NRSV).

Two Fundamental Ways of Being

At the center of the Arbinger framework is a deceptively simple distinction. There are, the research suggests, only two fundamental orientations from which a human being can relate to the people around them. These are not personality types or leadership styles — they are deeper than both. The Arbinger Institute (2016) calls them Mindsets.

The Outward Mindset is the orientation in which I see the people around me as full human beings — as individuals with their own needs, objectives, and challenges that are real and legitimate, independent of whether they serve my goals. From this mindset, I am genuinely curious about the pressures my colleague is navigating. I am moved by the burden my volunteer is carrying. I measure my own effectiveness not by whether I did what I intended to do, but by whether my actions actually made a positive difference for the people around me (Arbinger Institute, 2016). The Outward Mindset is not sentimentality. It is a disciplined, accountable posture of genuine other-orientation.

The Inward Mindset — what earlier Arbinger materials called being "In the Box" — is the orientation in which others exist primarily as instruments within my story. From this posture, other people register as one of three kinds of object: a vehicle (useful to my purposes), an obstacle (in the way of my purposes), or an irrelevancy (beneath notice entirely) (Arbinger Institute, 2000). The Inward Mindset does not make me a monster. It makes me ordinary. It is the default condition of a self that has not been deliberately, prayerfully cultivated in the direction of other-orientation.

What makes this distinction so practically important for ministry leaders is the finding that our Way of Being — which Mindset we are operating from in a given moment — is deeper and more determinative than our behavior (RDV Corporation, 2024). Two leaders can perform identical behaviors — the same meeting, the same words, the same initiative — while one is operating from an Outward Mindset and the other from an Inward Mindset. The people around them will know the difference, even if they cannot name it. They are, as Paul observed, responding not merely to what we say and do, but to who we are toward them (RDV Corporation, 2024).

Think About It

Before reading further: Think of a leader who made you feel genuinely seen — not as a role you filled, but as a person. What was different about how they related to you? Now think of a leader who, however competent, left you feeling like a means to an end. What did that feel like? How did it affect your engagement, your trust, your willingness to contribute your best?

How We Get "In the Box": Self-Betrayal and Its Consequences

The Arbinger framework offers a precise account of how an Inward Mindset takes root in a person who may be entirely well-intentioned. The mechanism is called self-betrayal — the moment when I have a genuine sense that I should do something helpful for another person, and I choose not to act on that sense (Arbinger Institute, 2000).

This is not a grand moral failure. It is the small, quotidian choice not to check in on a colleague who seemed weighed down in this morning's meeting. It is the decision not to offer a word of encouragement to a volunteer who is clearly discouraged. It is the impulse to stop and listen, immediately overridden by the competing priority of my own agenda. Self-betrayal is the seed from which all In-Box behavior grows — and it begins, characteristically, in a tiny and unremarkable moment (Arbinger Institute, 2000).

What happens after that small betrayal is deeply instructive. Once I have ignored my sense of what I should do for another person, I am now, psychologically, in an uncomfortable position. I have acted (or failed to act) in a way that contradicts my self-image as a caring, conscientious person. The mind's response to this discomfort is documented with precision by Aronson and Tavris (2007): it begins to reframe reality in ways that justify the choice it has already made. Within minutes of the self-betrayal, I will find myself seeing the other person differently. They become, in my inner narrative, less deserving of the consideration I withheld. I begin to horribilize them — unconsciously making them out to be worse than they actually are, more difficult, more ungrateful, more demanding (RDV Corporation, 2024). And I begin to attribute the relational friction I feel not to my own closed heart, but to their deficiencies.

Aronson and Tavris (2007) describe this process as cognitive dissonance reduction — the engine of self-justification. Through what they call "confirmation pyramids," small initial justifications compound into entrenched positions of blame and suspicion. The person who began by missing a chance to offer encouragement may find themselves, weeks later, genuinely believing that their colleague is self-absorbed, incompetent, or even hostile — a conclusion that conveniently explains and justifies every subsequent withdrawal of warmth or support.

The DVULI curriculum's adaptation of this framework is particularly attentive to what the Arbinger materials call Carry Boxes — the characteristic patterns of self-justification that each individual carries into their relationships over a lifetime (RDV Corporation, 2024). Some leaders go In the Box primarily through a better-than style: they see themselves as more capable, more committed, or more spiritually mature than those around them, and begin to treat others as subordinates in their personal hierarchy of worth. Others carry a must-be-seen-as box, deriving their sense of safety from the opinions of others, and responding to any perceived slight with a defensive hardening. Still others operate from a worse-than posture — seeing themselves as victims of others' indifference or cruelty, and recruiting allies to validate the injury they believe they have suffered (Arbinger Institute, 2006). In every case, the box is not primarily a strategy. It is a way of seeing that has become habitual and self-sustaining.

The Collusion Diagram: How Two "In-Box" People Build a System

Individual self-deception is damaging enough. But the Arbinger framework's most genuinely illuminating contribution to organizational analysis is its account of what happens when two people — or two groups — are simultaneously In the Box toward each other. This dynamic, called collusion, produces something qualitatively different from the sum of two difficult individuals. It produces a system (Arbinger Institute, 2000).

Consider a common ministry scenario: A senior pastor has grown impatient with what he perceives as his youth director's inability to follow through on commitments. From his In-Box perspective, she is unreliable and self-absorbed. His behavior toward her shifts — he stops sharing information proactively, gives her feedback only in large-group settings, and assigns her to lower-visibility responsibilities. From her perspective, his behavior is evidence that he neither respects her work nor trusts her judgment. She responds by withdrawing effort, protecting her own domain, and privately building her case with volunteers and peers. He observes her withdrawal as confirmation of his earlier judgment. She observes his exclusion as confirmation of hers. Each party's In-Box behavior is producing precisely the behavior in the other that justifies remaining In the Box (Arbinger Institute, 2006).

This is what the Arbinger Institute calls collusion — not a deliberate conspiracy, but a mutual, unconscious collaboration in conflict. Neither party sees themselves as generating the problem. Each sees themselves as responding, reasonably and even generously, to the other's provocations. The cycle is therefore self-sealing: every attempt to address the relationship from within the box — every defensive justification, every recruitment of allies, every attempt to "communicate" one's own grievances — serves to accelerate rather than interrupt the system (Arbinger Institute, 2000).

The ancient echoes of this dynamic run through Scripture. Cain's narrative in Genesis 4 is, among other things, a portrait of collusion's logic: the self-betrayal of a misdirected offering compounds into a horribilized view of Abel, which compounds into the first murder, which is then followed by a self-justifying denial (RDV Corporation, 2024). The Pharisees and Herodians in Mark 3 represent institutional collusion — two parties whose mutual antagonism has been entirely subordinated to the project of eliminating Jesus. Paul's epistles, particularly Galatians 5 and the early chapters of 1 Corinthians, document the collusion dynamics threatening the early church: parties "biting and devouring each other" in ways that guarantee mutual destruction, each faction certain that the fault lies entirely with the other (RDV Corporation, 2024).

For the ministry leader, the diagnostic question that the Collusion Diagram poses is as simple as it is confronting: In the relationships where I am most frustrated, what am I doing that gives the other person someone to respond to — someone who is closed, withdrawn, defensive, or critical? How am I producing the behaviors in them that I then use to justify my own In-Box posture?

The Imago Dei Foundation: Why This Is Not Just Psychology

Everything described above can be framed in purely psychological terms — cognitive dissonance, attribution bias, confirmation pyramids. The Arbinger framework itself does not rest on any single religious tradition; its principles have been validated across an enormous range of organizational and cultural contexts (Arbinger Institute, 2016). But for the DVULI leader, the Arbinger framework gains its deepest traction not from its social-scientific grounding alone, but from its resonance with a prior, more fundamental claim about the nature of other people.

That claim is the doctrine of the Imago Dei — the affirmation of Genesis 1:26-27 that every human being, without exception, is created in the image of God. In his magisterial treatment of this doctrine, Middleton (2005) argues against narrowly rationalist or moralistic readings. The Imago Dei is not primarily about human capacities for reason or moral choice. It designates the royal-functional identity of human beings as God's representatives and stewards within creation — beings who carry irreducible worth not because of what they achieve or produce, but because of what they are. Middleton (2005) further emphasizes that the Imago Dei is inherently relational and communal: it is not an attribute possessed by isolated individuals, but a dignity constituted and expressed in the context of community.

This has direct implications for the leader who moves into an Inward Mindset toward a colleague, a volunteer, or a youth. When I reduce another person to an obstacle or an irrelevancy — when I horribilize them or treat them as an instrument — I am not merely violating an ethical norm. I am, as the DVULI Believer's Guide puts it, creating "a fundamental barrier between ourselves and others… and between ourselves and God" (RDV Corporation, 2024). To deny the full personhood of another human being is to violate their status as an image-bearer of God. It is, in the language of this framework, to go to war — not merely relationally but ontologically — with the way God has designed the world to work.

The practical implication is significant: the Outward Mindset is not merely a more effective leadership strategy, though it is certainly that (Arbinger Institute, 2016). It is a posture that aligns with the grain of creation. The Inward Mindset, conversely, moves against that grain — and this is why it produces, reliably and systematically, the structural patterns of avoidance, passive aggression, and organizational stagnation that leaders like Marcus find baffling.

The Christological Pattern: Philippians 2 and Kenotic Leadership

The most demanding articulation of Outward Mindset leadership in the New Testament is found in Philippians 2:1–8, the passage Paul deploys to address a specific conflict within the Philippian community. Fee (1995), in his definitive NICNT commentary, argues that the Christ Hymn is not primarily a speculative Christological text. It is Paul's most radical statement about the shape of community ethics.

The Greek term ekenōsen — "he emptied himself" — describes Christ's voluntary relinquishment of the status and privileges of divine equality in order to take on the full vulnerability of human servanthood. This kenosis is not presented as loss. It is presented as the deepest expression of what it means to have the "same mind" (phronēte) that characterized Christ's own way of being in the world (Fee, 1995). When Paul appeals to this pattern in verses 1–4, he is not inviting the Philippians to aspire to an impossible ideal. He is calling them to participate in the same other-directed orientation that defines the Son of God's own posture toward humanity.

In the Arbinger framework's language, the Christ Hymn is a portrait of the Outward Mindset at its most radical. The one who had every possible justification for treating others as subordinates — for seeing himself as better-than, for insisting on recognition and deference — chose instead a posture of genuine seeing, genuine service, and genuine self-emptying on behalf of those who could offer nothing in return. As the DVULI Believer's Guide notes: "It is only by shifting our perspective from a focus on ourselves to a focus on others that we can create the unity — and safety — we all seek" (RDV Corporation, 2024, p. 4).

Think About It

Paul's appeal in Philippians 2:3–4 is specific: "Do nothing from selfish ambition or conceit, but in humility regard others as better than yourselves. Let each of you look not to your own interests, but to the interests of others." Where in your current ministry relationships does this sound like an impossibility? What would have to shift in your way of seeing — not just your behavior — for this posture to become genuine rather than performed?

Burden-Bearing Community: Galatians 6 and the Heart at Peace

Galatians 6:1–5 completes the biblical framework for this session. The passage is introduced early in DVULI's Pre-Assignment materials as a foundational text for the entire program (RDV Corporation, 2024): "Carry each other's burdens, and in this way you will fulfill the law of Christ." What this passage describes is not a program for heroic individuals. It describes the social condition that becomes possible when a community of people is operating from a collective Heart at Peace — when individuals have moved from an Inward Mindset in which others' burdens are obstacles to a Heart at Peace in which others' burdens are genuine calls upon my attention and care.

The counterweight in verses 4–5 — "each one should test their own actions" and "carry their own load" — is not a contradiction but a prerequisite. Burden-bearing community is only possible when individuals have first moved through the kind of honest self-examination that characterizes Outward Mindset leadership. The person who has not faced their own Carry Box patterns will inevitably project onto those they ostensibly "serve" — using acts of apparent generosity as instruments of subtle control or as evidence of their own superior virtue. The fruit of an Outward Mindset is community that is genuinely free — free to give and receive without agenda, without score-keeping, without the hidden ledgers that an Inward Mindset always maintains.

Greenleaf (1977) anticipated this dynamic in his foundational formulation of servant leadership. His central test — "Do those served grow as persons? Do they, while being served, become healthier, wiser, freer, more autonomous?" — is structurally equivalent to the Arbinger framework's most demanding accountability question: not "Did I try to help?" but "Did my actions actually make things better for this person?" (Arbinger Institute, 2016). Both frameworks converge on the same conviction: genuine leadership is measured not by the leader's intentions or efforts, but by the leader's actual impact on the people they serve.

The SAM Process: A Practical Path Back to a Heart at Peace

The Arbinger framework is not merely diagnostic. It provides a concrete, three-step discipline for recovering a Heart at Peace once the warning signs of an Inward Mindset have been recognized. This process, formalized in The Outward Mindset (Arbinger Institute, 2016), is summarized by the acronym SAM: See Others → Adjust Efforts → Measure Impact.

Step 1 — See Others

The first step is deceptively simple and deeply demanding: to genuinely seek to understand another person's needs, objectives, and challenges as they actually experience them. Not as I imagine they experience them through my In-Box filter — which by definition distorts and flattens the other person into a caricature that justifies my posture toward them — but as they actually experience them.

The empirical literature strongly supports the power of this move. Galinsky and Moskowitz (2000), in a landmark study published in the Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, demonstrated that deliberate perspective-taking — actively imagining another person's day-in-the-life — measurably reduces stereotyping, intergroup bias, and in-group favoritism. Participants who were instructed to take the perspective of an out-group member showed significantly more complex, individualized person-perception. The cognitive and attitudinal discipline of genuinely seeing another person produces measurable structural change in how we relate to them (Galinsky & Moskowitz, 2000). The SAM process begins here not as a courtesy, but as an act of moral and psychological recalibration.

Step 2 — Adjust Efforts

The second step is where insight becomes action. Based on what I have genuinely seen in Step 1, I ask three questions that the DVULI Believer's Guide presents as the practical heart of this phase (RDV Corporation, 2024):

  • How have I gotten in this person's way, or added to their challenges, trials, or burdens?
  • How have I — in large or small ways — mistreated or neglected them?
  • In light of the above, what is one specific thing I could and should do for them?

This is not a negotiation. The Inward Mindset will immediately generate objections: But they started it. But if I reach out first, I'll look weak. But I've tried before and it didn't work. The Arbinger framework is clear that these objections — however plausible they feel from inside the box — are produced by the box itself and cannot be trusted as accurate guides to action (Arbinger Institute, 2016). The only move that breaks the collusion cycle is an action that gives the other person a genuinely different person to respond to.

Step 3 — Measure Impact

The third step is what distinguishes the SAM process from a mere behavioral adjustment — and what makes it most challenging for leaders who are accustomed to evaluating themselves by their own efforts rather than their actual effects. The question of Step 3 is not Did I try to be more helpful? but Is my adjusted approach actually making a positive difference for this person? (Arbinger Institute, 2016).

This accountability orientation connects directly to DVULI's Core Value of Accountability — the commitment to honest introspection, the prayerful search for wisdom, and the willingness to seek and tell the truth in one's relationships (RDV Corporation, 2024). A leader who adjusts their efforts and then declares victory before waiting to observe their actual impact has merely replaced one form of self-focus with another. Measuring impact requires sustained humility — the ongoing willingness to ask the people around us whether what we are doing is genuinely helping them.

Think About It

The DVULI Believer's Guide closes its treatment of the SAM process with a reminder that is easy to forget: "DO what you have a sense to do to be helpful!" (RDV Corporation, 2024, p. 20). The process is not complete until action is taken. What specific action — however small — do you have a sense right now that you should take toward someone in your ministry with whom the relationship is strained?

The Iceberg Connection: Inward Mindset as the Deepest Mental Model

Returning now to the systems framework of Session 13: where does the Inward Mindset fit on the Systems Iceberg? The answer is instructive. The behaviors generated by an Inward Mindset — avoidance, passive aggression, silo-building, passive resistance to leadership initiatives — are events visible at the surface of the iceberg. The patterns that result from these behaviors — declining team cohesion, stagnating programs, accelerating volunteer turnover — are the iceberg's second level. The structural habits that institutionalize these patterns — the unwritten rules that discourage honest disagreement, the meeting norms that reward compliance over candor — are the third level.

But the Inward Mindset itself — the habitual tendency to see others as objects rather than as image-bearers — operates at the fourth level: the level of mental models. It is, in the Arbinger framework's terms, a Way of Being that is deeper and more determinative than any conscious belief about strategy or theology (Arbinger Institute, 2016). It is, in the iceberg's language, the foundational assumption that governs all the structures and patterns above it. This is why no structural intervention — however well-designed — can produce lasting relational health in an organization whose core mental model is Inward. The structures will be silently colonized by the mindset until they reproduce the same patterns they were designed to interrupt.

For the DVULI leader, this means that the work of Session 14 is not supplementary to the systems work of Session 13. It is the foundation of it. The leader who cannot navigate their own Carry Box patterns, who cannot interrupt their own collusion cycles, who cannot move from a Heart at War to a Heart at Peace in their closest and most challenging relationships — that leader cannot sustain an organization characterized by the Outward Mindset, because organizations are ultimately constituted by the ways of being of the people within them.

Interactive Workshop Activities

🔍 In-Box Warning Signs Self-Assessment
Select a specific difficult relationship you are currently navigating. Rate each of the 20 warning signs below. Then toggle to a second relationship to compare your patterns. Your "Carry Box Profile" will reveal your characteristic self-justifying patterns — with a Scripture-anchored reflection question for each.
🔄 Collusion Diagram Mapper
Map a specific conflict relationship using the five steps below. Click "Animate My Collusion Cycle" to see how each action feeds the next — and to generate three Stop-the-Loop questions for your situation.

⛔ Three Stop-the-Loop Questions

🌱 SAM Process Interactive Guide
Apply the See → Adjust → Measure framework to the same difficult relationship. Complete all three steps, then identify your 48-hour first action and your accountability partner.
Step 1 — See Others
"Strive to understand the other person's needs, objectives, and challenges." (Arbinger Institute, 2016) Write three things you now believe to be true about this person that you had not previously considered — especially pressures, burdens, or fears they carry.
Step 2 — Adjust Efforts
Based on what you now see, identify one specific thing you have been doing that gets in this person's way — and one concrete action you could take that would genuinely help them.
Step 3 — Measure Impact
"The real test of our effectiveness is whether it makes a positive difference for the other person." (RDV Corporation, 2024) How will you know if your adjusted approach is working? Who will you ask for honest feedback?
My 48-Hour Commitment
The SAM process is only complete when action is taken (RDV Corporation, 2024). Identify the first specific action you will take within 48 hours, and name the person who will know about your commitment — embedding accountability directly into your practice of the Outward Mindset.

✅ Your SAM Guide Is Complete

You have moved through all three steps of the Outward Mindset Pattern. The commitments you have made above are the beginning of a Heart at Peace in action. Review them before each interaction with this person over the next 30 days. Remember the Psalmist's invitation in Psalm 139:23–24: "Search me, O God, and know my heart... See if there is any wicked way in me, and lead me in the way everlasting." Accountability works best as a team sport — tell someone today.

Key Takeaways

  • The most pervasive source of organizational breakdown in ministry is not structural or strategic but relational: the degree to which leaders and team members have closed their hearts toward one another and are operating from an Inward Mindset (Arbinger Institute, 2016).
  • Self-betrayal — the small, habitual choice not to act on what I sense I should do for another person — is the mechanism by which an Inward Mindset takes root and compounds into entrenched patterns of blame and horribilization (Arbinger Institute, 2000; Aronson & Tavris, 2007).
  • Collusion is not a deliberate conspiracy but a mutual, unconscious collaboration in conflict: each party's In-Box behavior produces the exact behavior in the other that justifies remaining In the Box, creating a self-sealing system that no behavioral intervention can reach from within (Arbinger Institute, 2006).
  • The Imago Dei doctrine provides the theological foundation for the Outward Mindset: every person in any organization, regardless of role or performance, carries irreducible worth as a bearer of God's image, and to reduce them to an object is to violate not merely ethics but ontology (Middleton, 2005).
  • The SAM process — See Others, Adjust Efforts, Measure Impact — provides a concrete, accountable discipline for recovering a Heart at Peace and interrupting collusion cycles in specific relationships (Arbinger Institute, 2016; RDV Corporation, 2024).
  • The Inward Mindset is the deepest mental model on the Systems Iceberg: structural interventions cannot produce lasting relational health in an organization whose core way of being is inward-facing. Transformation must begin at the level of how leaders see people (Arbinger Institute, 2016).
  • Philippians 2:1–8 presents the kenotic self-emptying of Christ as the Christological pattern for Outward Mindset leadership — a calling not merely to better behavior but to the same "mind" (phronēte) that characterized Jesus's own other-directed way of being (Fee, 1995).

Looking Ahead

Session 15 moves from the interior dynamics of the leader's relationships to the exterior horizon of breakthrough planning. Having examined the deepest mental model on the iceberg — the way of being that either releases or blocks genuine organizational transformation — you will now integrate your personal and ministry learning journals into concrete Focus Statements and action plans. The Outward Mindset work of this session is not merely a personal development exercise: it is the relational foundation without which no Breakthrough Plan, however well-constructed, can sustain itself over time. Come to the next session having named at least one specific collusion you will interrupt and one SAM process you have begun.

items.question: According to the Arbinger framework, what is the fundamental difference between an Outward Mindset and an Inward Mindset?
items.correctAnswer: An Outward Mindset sees others as people with inherent dignity, while an Inward Mindset sees others as objects in one's own story
items.correctFeedback: The foundational distinction in the Arbinger framework is between two 'Ways of Being.' The Outward Mindset recognizes others as full, complex, dignified human beings with their own needs, objectives, and challenges. The Inward Mindset reduces others to objects — instruments of my goals, threats to my security, or irrelevancies to my mission.

distractors.text: An Outward Mindset focuses on external strategy and programs, while an Inward Mindset focuses on personal spiritual growth
distractors.feedback: Students might confuse 'outward' and 'inward' with external activities versus internal spiritual life. However, these terms describe how we see other people, not whether our focus is on programs or personal devotion. A leader can be deeply spiritual yet still operate from an Inward Mindset toward colleagues.

distractors.text: An Outward Mindset is extroverted and public-facing, while an Inward Mindset is introverted and reflective
distractors.feedback: This confuses personality traits with ways of being. The Outward/Inward distinction has nothing to do with introversion or extroversion. An introverted leader can operate with a deeply Outward Mindset, and an extroverted leader can be thoroughly 'in the box' toward others.

distractors.text: An Outward Mindset prioritizes the organization's mission above all else, while an Inward Mindset prioritizes personal comfort and self-protection
distractors.feedback: While personal comfort can be a factor in an Inward Mindset, the distinction is not about mission versus comfort. A leader can aggressively pursue organizational mission while still treating people as objects — as vehicles for accomplishing that mission. The key issue is whether others are seen as people, not whether the mission is prioritized.

items.question: In the Arbinger framework, what are the three ways a person operating from an Inward Mindset might see others as objects?
items.correctAnswer: As obstacles to overcome, vehicles to use, or irrelevancies to ignore
items.correctFeedback: The Inward Mindset reduces people to three categories of objects: obstacles (people who get in the way of what I want), vehicles (people I can use to accomplish my goals), and irrelevancies (people who don't matter to my purposes). Each of these denies the full humanity and dignity of the other person.

distractors.text: As enemies to defeat, allies to recruit, or strangers to avoid
distractors.feedback: While this captures some relational dynamics, it reflects common interpersonal categories rather than the specific Arbinger objectification framework. The Arbinger terms — obstacles, vehicles, irrelevancies — are more precise because they describe how the Inward Mindset turns people into functional objects rather than recognizing them as people.

distractors.text: As problems to solve, resources to manage, or distractions to eliminate
distractors.feedback: This language sounds similar but reframes the categories in management terms rather than the Arbinger framework's specific terminology. The distinction matters because 'obstacles, vehicles, and irrelevancies' emphasize that the issue is objectification of persons, not a management style problem.

distractors.text: As sinners to correct, servants to direct, or outsiders to convert
distractors.feedback: Students might select this because it sounds relevant to a ministry context. However, these are not the Arbinger categories. The framework uses obstacles, vehicles, and irrelevancies as universal categories of objectification that apply in any context, not specifically religious roles.

items.question: What does the Arbinger framework mean by the term 'self-betrayal'?
items.correctAnswer: Failing to act on a genuine sense of what one should do for another person
items.correctFeedback: Self-betrayal is the specific moment when a person has an authentic sense that they should do something for another person — help, listen, acknowledge, serve — and chooses not to follow through. This act of betraying one's own sense of right action toward another is the entry point into 'the box,' triggering the cascade of self-justification that follows.

distractors.text: Compromising one's personal values to please others in the organization
distractors.feedback: Students might assume self-betrayal means sacrificing personal convictions. However, in the Arbinger framework, self-betrayal is the opposite — it is the failure to act on what you genuinely sense you should do for another person. It is betraying the impulse toward others, not betraying your own preferences.

distractors.text: Deceiving oneself into believing that one is more competent than one actually is
distractors.feedback: While self-deception is related to self-betrayal, this answer conflates the two. Self-deception is the broader condition of not seeing reality clearly. Self-betrayal is the specific triggering act — not following through on what you sense you should do for another person — that initiates the self-deceptive cycle.

distractors.text: Publicly advocating for a ministry vision while privately doubting its effectiveness
distractors.feedback: This describes a form of hypocrisy or internal doubt, but it is not what Arbinger means by self-betrayal. Self-betrayal specifically concerns the moment you sense you should do something for another person and choose not to act. It is about failing a person, not about doubting a program.

items.question: Which biblical doctrine does the session identify as the theological foundation for why every person must be seen through an Outward Mindset?
items.correctAnswer: Imago Dei — that every person bears the image of God and possesses inherent dignity
items.correctFeedback: The Imago Dei doctrine teaches that every human being is created in the image of God, which gives each person inherent, non-negotiable dignity. This provides the theological grounding for the Outward Mindset: if every person bears God's image, then reducing anyone to an obstacle, vehicle, or irrelevancy is not merely a leadership failure — it is a violation of their God-given worth.

distractors.text: Total Depravity — that every person is fallen and therefore in need of grace from others
distractors.feedback: While Total Depravity is a real theological doctrine that acknowledges human fallenness, using it as the foundation for seeing others would emphasize what is broken in people, not their inherent dignity. The session grounds the Outward Mindset in Imago Dei precisely because it affirms the worth and dignity every person carries regardless of their behavior.

distractors.text: Justification by Faith — that every person is declared righteous through belief in Christ
distractors.feedback: Justification by Faith is a core Protestant doctrine about salvation, but it applies specifically to believers and addresses legal standing before God. Imago Dei, by contrast, applies to every human being — believer or not — and establishes the universal dignity that the Outward Mindset must honor.

distractors.text: The Great Commission — that every person is a potential disciple who must be reached
distractors.feedback: Students in ministry might default to the Great Commission as a reason to value others. However, seeing people primarily as potential converts could actually be an Inward Mindset posture — treating people as vehicles for accomplishing a mission goal. Imago Dei establishes dignity as inherent, not contingent on someone's usefulness to a ministry objective.

items.question: A youth pastor notices a volunteer consistently arriving late but feels prompted to ask if everything is okay at home. Instead, the pastor ignores the impulse, says nothing, and begins mentally labeling the volunteer as 'irresponsible.' According to the Arbinger framework, what has occurred?
items.correctAnswer: The pastor committed self-betrayal by not acting on the impulse, then entered the box and began horribilizing the volunteer
items.correctFeedback: This is a textbook illustration of the self-betrayal sequence. The pastor had a genuine sense of what to do for the volunteer (ask about their well-being), betrayed that impulse, and then entered a self-justifying posture — 'the box' — that includes horribilizing (making the volunteer worse than they are by labeling them 'irresponsible'). The label protects the pastor from feeling the weight of their own inaction.

distractors.text: The pastor exercised appropriate boundaries by not getting involved in the volunteer's personal life
distractors.feedback: While professional boundaries are important, the Arbinger framework would identify the pastor's specific internal shift — from genuine concern to dismissive labeling — as evidence that something deeper has happened. The issue is not whether to intervene but that the pastor betrayed an authentic impulse toward the person and then justified it by reducing the volunteer to a negative label.

distractors.text: The pastor engaged in collusion by creating a mutual conflict system with the volunteer
distractors.feedback: Collusion requires both parties to be simultaneously 'in the box' toward each other, creating a self-reinforcing cycle. In this scenario, we only see the pastor's internal process. The volunteer may not yet be in the box toward the pastor. This is self-betrayal leading to being in the box, which could eventually lead to collusion — but collusion has not yet been established.

distractors.text: The pastor completed the SAM process but applied it incorrectly to the situation
distractors.feedback: The SAM process — See Others, Adjust Efforts, Measure Impact — is the path out of the box, not into it. The pastor did the opposite of SAM: they failed to truly see the volunteer's needs, did not adjust their efforts to help, and made no attempt to measure impact. SAM was never initiated in this scenario.

items.question: What does the Collusion Diagram in the Arbinger framework reveal about conflict between two people or groups?
items.correctAnswer: Each party's 'in the box' behavior provokes the very response in the other that justifies staying in the box, creating a self-perpetuating system
items.correctFeedback: The Collusion Diagram makes visible one of the most destructive dynamics in organizations: when two people or groups are simultaneously in the box toward each other, each acts in ways that produce the very behaviors in the other that seem to justify their own hostile or dismissive posture. This creates a closed, self-reinforcing system where both parties feel entirely justified while both are contributing to the problem.

distractors.text: One party is always the primary aggressor and the other is always the victim responding defensively, a dynamic that becomes entrenched through repeated cycles of blame and retaliation
distractors.feedback: Students may want to assign clear blame in conflict, but the Collusion Diagram's key insight is precisely that both parties are co-creating the destructive system. There is no innocent victim and guilty aggressor — both are in the box, both are contributing to the cycle, and both bear responsibility for the system they are sustaining together.

distractors.text: Conflict arises primarily from structural misalignment in organizational roles and responsibilities
distractors.feedback: While structural issues can contribute to conflict, the Collusion Diagram specifically reveals a relational-systemic dynamic rooted in both parties' ways of being. The session explicitly argues that the greatest systemic problem is not structural or strategic but is the degree to which people have closed their hearts toward one another.

distractors.text: External pressures such as limited resources force otherwise healthy teams into adversarial postures
distractors.feedback: This answer locates the source of conflict in external circumstances. The Collusion Diagram, however, shows that the conflict is internally generated and self-sustaining. External pressures may be real, but the collusion system persists because of the internal posture — the way of being — of each party toward the other, not because of resource scarcity.

items.question: What are the three steps of the SAM process, and what is their intended purpose?
items.correctAnswer: See Others (understand their needs, objectives, and challenges), Adjust Efforts (change actions to be more genuinely helpful), and Measure Impact (assess whether actions actually made things better for the other person)
items.correctFeedback: The SAM process is the practical path back to a Heart at Peace. 'See Others' means genuinely understanding another person's needs, objectives, and challenges — not as you imagine them but as they actually are. 'Adjust Efforts' means changing your behavior based on what you now see. 'Measure Impact' means holding yourself accountable not for your intentions but for whether your actions actually improved things for the other person.

distractors.text: Set goals (define personal ministry objectives), Act decisively (implement plans with confidence), and Monitor progress (track outcomes against benchmarks)
distractors.feedback: This sounds like a standard management process, but it is self-referential — focused on the leader's own goals and progress. The SAM process is fundamentally other-oriented: it begins with seeing the other person's reality, not setting your own goals. This distinction is crucial because an Inward Mindset can efficiently set goals and track progress while still treating people as objects.

distractors.text: Surrender pride (confess wrongdoing), Ask forgiveness (seek reconciliation), and Make amends (restore the relationship through reparative action)
distractors.feedback: While these are valuable spiritual practices, they describe a reconciliation process, not the SAM framework. The SAM process is proactive and ongoing — it is a way of operating daily, not only a response to past wrongs. It begins with seeing the other person, not with confessing one's own failures.

distractors.text: Study Scripture (ground decisions in biblical truth), Apply principles (live out the insights gained), and Mentor others (pass on lessons learned to the next generation)
distractors.feedback: Students in a ministry context might gravitate toward this spiritual formation answer. However, the SAM process is specifically about one's orientation toward other people in daily interactions — seeing their reality, adjusting behavior to help them, and measuring whether it actually helped. It is a relational accountability tool, not a discipleship curriculum.

items.question: How does the session connect the Inward Mindset to the systems thinking iceberg model introduced in earlier DVULI sessions?
items.correctAnswer: The Inward Mindset operates as a mental model at the deepest level of the iceberg, producing structural patterns like avoidance and silos that generate surface-level events like conflict and turnover
items.correctFeedback: The session explicitly integrates the Arbinger framework with systems thinking by positioning the Inward Mindset at the bottom of the iceberg — the level of mental models. From that invisible foundation, structural patterns emerge (avoidance, silos, passive aggression), which in turn produce the visible behavioral events organizations actually notice (conflict, staff turnover, ministry stagnation). This connection shows why addressing only surface events never resolves the deeper issue.

distractors.text: The Inward Mindset is an event-level phenomenon that can be corrected through targeted behavioral interventions like conflict resolution workshops or communication training
distractors.feedback: Placing the Inward Mindset at the event level fundamentally misunderstands both the iceberg model and the Arbinger framework. The session argues that the Inward Mindset is the deepest layer — a mental model — not a surface behavior. Treating it as an event leads to superficial interventions that never address the underlying way of being.

distractors.text: The iceberg model shows that the Inward Mindset is caused by structural deficiencies in the organization, so fixing structures will automatically shift mindsets
distractors.feedback: This reverses the causal direction the session describes. The iceberg model positions mental models as driving structures, not the other way around. While structural changes can help, the session argues that if the underlying Inward Mindset is not addressed, new structures will eventually reproduce the same dysfunctional patterns.

distractors.text: The Inward Mindset is a trend-level pattern that emerges only when multiple conflict events accumulate over time
distractors.feedback: The trend level of the iceberg describes patterns of behavior over time, not the underlying mindset that causes them. The Inward Mindset is deeper than trends — it is the mental model that generates the trends. Repeated conflicts are evidence of the Inward Mindset, not the Inward Mindset itself.

items.question: A ministry team leader implements the 'Adjust Efforts' step of the SAM process by reorganizing the weekly schedule to accommodate a team member's childcare challenges. However, the team member's stress level does not improve. According to the SAM framework, what should the leader do next?
items.correctAnswer: Engage the 'Measure Impact' step by evaluating whether the schedule change actually addressed the team member's real needs, and return to 'See Others' to understand the situation more accurately
items.correctFeedback: The Measure Impact step exists precisely for this situation — it holds the leader accountable not for good intentions but for actual results. When the adjustment does not produce the desired impact, the SAM process is iterative: the leader should go back to 'See Others' to understand what they may have missed about the team member's actual needs, objectives, and challenges, then adjust again based on deeper understanding.

distractors.text: Conclude that the team member is ungrateful and move on to helping other team members who are more responsive
distractors.feedback: This response reveals a return to the Inward Mindset — the leader is now seeing the team member as an irrelevancy or obstacle. The SAM process requires persistent other-orientation. When an adjustment fails, the framework calls for deeper seeing and further adjustment, not for labeling the person and withdrawing effort.

distractors.text: Repeat the same schedule adjustment with greater emphasis, since consistent effort will eventually produce results
distractors.feedback: Simply doing more of the same without re-examining whether you have accurately understood the other person's needs misses the iterative nature of SAM. The 'Measure Impact' step exists to create a feedback loop. If the impact was not positive, the answer is not more of the same effort — it is returning to 'See Others' to gain a more accurate understanding.

distractors.text: Confront the team member about their lack of improvement and require them to articulate what they actually need, while documenting the conversation for performance review purposes
distractors.feedback: While communication is important, framing this as a confrontation places the burden on the team member rather than on the leader's responsibility to see more clearly. The SAM process is leader-initiated: the leader takes responsibility for understanding the other person's reality rather than demanding that the other person make their needs easier to address.

items.question: Two department heads in a ministry organization are locked in an ongoing dispute. Each believes the other is undermining their work, each responds with defensive behavior that the other interprets as hostile, and each feels entirely justified in their stance. A consultant trained in the Arbinger framework would most likely identify this as an example of which concept, and why does addressing only one party's behavior fail to resolve it?
items.correctAnswer: Collusion — because the system is mutually reinforcing, with each party's in-the-box behavior provoking the very responses that justify the other's in-the-box posture, so changing one person's external behavior without shifting both parties' ways of being leaves the system intact
items.correctFeedback: This scenario is a classic Collusion Diagram in action. The reason addressing only one party's behavior fails is that collusion is a system, not a sequence of individual actions. Even if one person changes their external behavior, as long as the underlying way of being remains inward, the other party will detect the inauthenticity and the system will reconstitute itself. Both parties must shift from an Inward to an Outward way of being for the system to genuinely change.

distractors.text: Self-betrayal — because one of the department heads originally failed to act on an impulse to help the other, and resolving the original betrayal would resolve the current conflict
distractors.feedback: Self-betrayal may have been the initial trigger for one or both parties entering the box, but the current dynamic has evolved into something larger. Collusion is a systemic pattern that takes on a life of its own. Simply identifying the original self-betrayal will not dismantle the mutually reinforcing system that now exists between both parties.

distractors.text: Horribilizing — because both department heads are exaggerating each other's flaws, and correcting their perceptions through objective data will resolve the dispute
distractors.feedback: Horribilizing is indeed happening — both parties are making the other worse than they are. However, horribilizing is a symptom of being in the box, not the systemic dynamic itself. Providing objective data will not resolve the conflict because when people are in the box, they interpret even accurate data through their self-justifying lens. The underlying collusion system must be addressed.

distractors.text: Structural misalignment — because the department heads have overlapping responsibilities, and clarifying organizational roles would eliminate the friction between them
distractors.feedback: Organizational clarity can reduce friction, but the session argues that the deepest source of conflict is not structural — it is the way of being with which people inhabit those structures. Two department heads operating with an Outward Mindset could navigate overlapping responsibilities collaboratively. The collusion system would likely persist even with perfectly clear role definitions because the problem is relational, not structural.

questions.stem: In the Arbinger framework, a leader operating from an Inward Mindset sees other people primarily as:
options: Objects — obstacles, vehicles, or irrelevancies to their own goals
Teammates — partners who share identical objectives
Subordinates — people who need direction and correction
Competitors — rivals who threaten the leader's position

feedback.correct: The Inward Mindset reduces people to objects in three specific ways: as obstacles blocking what I want, vehicles I can use to get what I want, or irrelevancies I can ignore. This is the core distortion that creates a Heart at War.
feedback.incorrect: The framework isn't about hierarchy or competition. The key distinction is between seeing others as full human beings (Outward Mindset) versus reducing them to objects in your personal story — whether as obstacles, vehicles, or irrelevancies.

variants.stem: According to the Arbinger framework, what is the fundamental difference between an Outward Mindset and an Inward Mindset?
options: An Outward Mindset sees others as people with inherent dignity; an Inward Mindset sees others as objects in one's own story
An Outward Mindset focuses on external results; an Inward Mindset focuses on internal spiritual growth
An Outward Mindset is extroverted leadership; an Inward Mindset is introverted leadership
An Outward Mindset delegates tasks; an Inward Mindset controls tasks directly

feedback.correct: The Outward Mindset sees others as people — full, complex, dignified human beings with their own needs, objectives, and challenges. The Inward Mindset collapses others into objects: obstacles, vehicles, or irrelevancies in one's own narrative.
feedback.incorrect: The Outward/Inward distinction is not about personality type, delegation style, or focus area. It is about the fundamental way you see other human beings — as people with inherent dignity or as objects serving your narrative.

questions.stem: In the Arbinger framework, 'self-betrayal' occurs when a leader:
options: Has a sense of something they should do for another person but acts against that sense
Publicly promises a change in behavior but privately continues the old pattern
Confesses a sin to an accountability partner and then repeats it
Recognizes a systemic problem but lacks the authority to address it

feedback.correct: Self-betrayal is the precise moment you betray your own sense of what you should do for another person. This act of going against what you know is right toward someone else is the trigger that puts you 'in the box' and shifts you into an Inward Mindset.
feedback.incorrect: Self-betrayal in this framework is not about broken promises or repeated sins in the conventional sense. It is specifically about betraying your instinct to respond to another person as a person — the moment you feel you should help, listen, or acknowledge someone and choose not to.

variants.stem: A youth ministry director notices a volunteer looking overwhelmed after an event. She feels she should ask if the volunteer is okay, but instead walks past to check on the budget report. According to the Arbinger framework, this is an example of:
options: Self-betrayal — acting against her sense of what she should do for another person
Time management — appropriately prioritizing Quadrant I tasks
Delegation failure — not assigning emotional care to another leader
Structural misalignment — a systems problem beyond her control

feedback.correct: This is the classic self-betrayal pattern: the director felt a pull to respond to the volunteer as a person but chose instead to act as though the volunteer was an irrelevancy compared to the budget. This is the moment she goes 'in the box.'
feedback.incorrect: While time management and delegation are real leadership considerations, the Arbinger framework identifies something deeper here: the director betrayed her own felt sense of what the volunteer needed from her as a human being. This is self-betrayal, the entry point to the Inward Mindset.

questions.stem: Two department leaders in a church have stopped communicating. Pastor A believes the worship leader is 'territorial and uncooperative.' The worship leader believes Pastor A is 'controlling and dismissive.' Each leader's frustration with the other keeps growing. According to the Arbinger framework, what is the most accurate diagnosis of this situation?
options: They are in a collusion pattern — each person's in-the-box behavior provokes the very response in the other that justifies staying in the box
One leader is clearly in the box and the other is simply reacting to genuinely bad behavior
This is a structural problem that requires clearer role definitions and reporting lines
Both leaders need better conflict-resolution communication skills
justifications: Collusion is a self-reinforcing system: when both people are in the box, each person's defensive or aggressive behavior produces exactly the behavior they complain about in the other, creating a feedback loop of mutual justification
Collusion happens because one person started the conflict and the other person is simply defending themselves from genuine mistreatment
Collusion is primarily a communication breakdown that can be fixed by improving how each person expresses their feelings
Collusion only occurs between people of equal organizational authority; this is actually a hierarchical power struggle

feedback.correct: The Collusion Diagram reveals that this is a system, not a one-sided problem. Pastor A's controlling behavior provokes the worship leader's resistance, which confirms Pastor A's view that the worship leader is uncooperative, which justifies more controlling behavior — and the cycle runs in both directions simultaneously.
feedback.incorrect: It's tempting to see one person as the 'real' problem or to attribute the conflict to structure or skill deficits. But the Arbinger framework shows that when both parties are in the box, they are co-creating the system. Structural fixes and communication training will fail if both leaders remain in a Heart at War.
feedback.wrongReason: Students often correctly identify collusion but misunderstand its mechanism. Collusion is not about who started it or about communication style — it is a systemic feedback loop where each person's in-the-box behavior literally produces the other person's problematic behavior, making the system self-perpetuating.

variants.stem: A DVULI participant complains that her co-leader 'never takes initiative.' In response, she micromanages every detail. The co-leader, feeling distrusted, withdraws further and stops volunteering ideas. The DVULI participant sees this withdrawal as proof that the co-leader is passive. What does the Arbinger framework call this dynamic?
options: A collusion pattern — each leader's behavior provokes the exact response in the other that justifies their own in-the-box stance
A leadership competency gap — the co-leader genuinely lacks initiative and needs coaching
A personality mismatch — the two leaders have incompatible working styles
A structural problem — their roles and responsibilities are not clearly defined
justifications: The DVULI participant's micromanaging creates an environment where initiative is punished, which produces the co-leader's passivity, which then justifies more micromanaging — a self-reinforcing system where both are in the box
The co-leader's withdrawal proves that the DVULI participant's assessment was correct all along and she simply needs a more proactive team member
Collusion means both people are equally at fault and should simply apologize to each other to resolve the conflict
This pattern will resolve itself once the structural roles are clarified, since the real issue is ambiguity about who does what

feedback.correct: This is a textbook collusion pattern. The micromanaging creates the very passivity that is being complained about. Both leaders are in the box — one seeing the other as an obstacle (passive), the other seeing the first as an obstacle (controlling) — and each is producing the behavior that keeps the other in the box.
feedback.incorrect: It's easy to see the co-leader as the problem, but notice how the DVULI participant's behavior is manufacturing the outcome she fears. The Arbinger framework reveals that you cannot diagnose the other person's behavior apart from how your own in-the-box stance is shaping the system.
feedback.wrongReason: Some students recognize collusion but think it means 'both people need to apologize.' Collusion is deeper than that — it is a systemic dynamic where the very behaviors each person uses to cope with or correct the other are what produce the problem. Getting out of the box requires seeing the other as a person, not just saying sorry.

questions.stem: Evaluate the relationship between these two statements:
questions.assertion: The Outward Mindset requires leaders to see every person — including opponents and difficult team members — as having inherent dignity and worth.
questions.reason: The Imago Dei doctrine teaches that every human being bears the image of God, regardless of their behavior or role in an organization.

feedback.correct: Both statements are true, and the Imago Dei doctrine is the theological foundation that explains why the Outward Mindset is not merely a management technique but a moral and spiritual obligation. Because every person is an image-bearer of God, reducing anyone to an object (obstacle, vehicle, or irrelevancy) is a violation of their God-given dignity.
feedback.incorrect: Some students think the Outward Mindset is a pragmatic leadership strategy independent of theology. In this curriculum, however, the Imago Dei doctrine is the explicit reason leaders must see others as people — it is the theological ground that makes the Outward Mindset a matter of faithfulness, not just effectiveness.

variants.stem: Evaluate the relationship between these two statements:
variants.assertion: The SAM process (See Others, Adjust Efforts, Measure Impact) is the practical path for restoring a Heart at Peace.
variants.reason: Philippians 2:1-8 calls believers to the same self-emptying love that Christ demonstrated by taking the form of a servant.

feedback.correct: Both statements are true, and the Philippians passage provides the biblical model that the SAM process operationalizes. The SAM process is not merely a technique — it is the practical expression of Christlike self-emptying love: genuinely seeing others' needs, adjusting your behavior for their sake, and measuring whether your actions truly serve them.
feedback.incorrect: Students sometimes treat the SAM process as a secular business tool and the Philippians passage as unrelated devotional material. In this curriculum, the biblical text provides the theological motivation and model for the SAM process — they are deeply connected, not independent ideas.

questions.stem: After going 'in the box' through self-betrayal, the Arbinger framework describes a specific psychological process that keeps a person trapped in the Inward Mindset. This process involves:
options: Horribilizing the other person (making them worse than they are) and blaming them for problems the leader's own behavior is helping to create
Rationalizing one's own behavior by comparing it favorably to secular leadership standards rather than biblical ones
Suppressing emotional responses until they erupt in a single moment of confrontation
Gradually distancing from all relationships to protect oneself from further betrayal

feedback.correct: Once in the box, the self-justification machine kicks in: you horribilize the other person (inflating their faults, ignoring their humanity) and blame them for the very problems your in-the-box behavior is co-creating. This is how self-deception becomes self-perpetuating — your distorted view of the other person feels completely justified to you.
feedback.incorrect: The Arbinger framework identifies a specific two-part mechanism: horribilizing (making the other person worse than they are in your mind) and blaming (attributing your problems to their deficiencies). This is not about emotional suppression or general relational withdrawal — it is an active distortion of how you see the other person.

variants.stem: A ministry leader betrays her sense that she should listen to a frustrated team member. According to the Arbinger framework, what happens psychologically AFTER this self-betrayal?
options: She begins to inflate the team member's faults and blame them for problems she is helping to create, which justifies her staying in the box
She experiences immediate guilt that motivates her to apologize within a few hours
She unconsciously seeks out a third party to mediate the conflict
She shifts into a task-focused leadership mode that is emotionally neutral

feedback.correct: Self-betrayal triggers a self-justification cycle: the leader will begin horribilizing the team member (exaggerating faults, minimizing strengths) and blaming them for problems. This distorted perception feels true from inside the box, which is precisely why the framework calls it self-deception.
feedback.incorrect: The Arbinger framework does not predict guilt-driven repair or neutral task focus. Instead, self-betrayal activates a distortion process: the leader's view of the other person warps to justify the betrayal, making the other person look worse and the leader's own behavior look reasonable.

questions.stem: This session explicitly connects the Inward Mindset to the Systems Iceberg model from Session 13. At which level of the iceberg does the Inward Mindset primarily operate?
options: Mental Models — the deepest assumptions and beliefs that shape how we see reality
Events — the visible behavioral incidents that people notice and react to
Patterns — the recurring trends that emerge over time
Structures — the organizational policies and role definitions that shape behavior

feedback.correct: The Inward Mindset is itself a mental model — the deepest level of the iceberg below the waterline. It is a fundamental way of seeing others that produces structural patterns (avoidance, silos, passive aggression) which then generate visible events (conflict, turnover, stagnation). This is why surface-level fixes fail: the mental model keeps regenerating the problem.
feedback.incorrect: While the Inward Mindset produces effects at every level of the iceberg, it lives at the mental models level — the deep, often invisible assumptions about other people that shape everything above. Conflict events and avoidance patterns are symptoms; the Inward Mindset is the root cause operating below the waterline.

variants.stem: Session 14 argues that the Inward Mindset is 'the personal application of the systems thinking from Session 13.' According to the Iceberg model, the structural patterns that the Inward Mindset produces include:
options: Avoidance, silos, and passive aggression — recurring organizational behaviors that flow from seeing others as objects
Mission statements, org charts, and budget allocations — the formal frameworks of the organization
A single conflict event between two specific leaders at a particular meeting
The stated theological convictions of the organization's founding documents

feedback.correct: The Inward Mindset (mental model level) produces structural patterns like avoidance, silos, and passive aggression. These are not formal organizational structures like org charts — they are recurring relational dynamics that emerge when leaders see each other as objects rather than people.
feedback.incorrect: In the Systems Iceberg, structures are not just formal organizational elements. They include the informal relational patterns — avoidance, silo formation, passive aggression — that reliably emerge when the underlying mental model is an Inward Mindset. These patterns then generate the visible events (conflicts, turnover) that leaders typically try to fix.

questions.stem: A DVULI participant returns from training excited about a new outreach strategy. Rather than equipping and mobilizing her team, she implements the entire plan herself, working late nights and weekends. Within two months, she is exhausted and frustrated that 'nobody else cares.' According to the Week 7 framework, what is the primary problem?
options: She has fallen into the Action Hero trap — substituting individual performance for the process of identifying, equipping, mobilizing, and supporting others
She chose the wrong strategy — a better outreach plan would have generated more volunteer interest
She lacks the spiritual gift of administration and should delegate all planning to someone else
Her team members are not committed enough to the church's mission
justifications: The Action Hero problem occurs when a leader's instinct for individual performance undermines collective ministry capacity; the solution is the empowerment process of identifying gifts, equipping people, mobilizing them into action, and supporting them ongoing
The Action Hero problem is primarily about choosing strategies that are too ambitious for a small team to handle
The empowerment process requires the leader to stop doing all ministry work and only serve in an advisory capacity
Interdependence means every team member should be equally involved in every decision from the beginning

feedback.correct: This is the classic Action Hero trap from Week 7. Her individual performance instinct bypassed the empowerment process entirely. She needed to identify team members' gifts, equip them with skills and vision, mobilize them into specific roles, and support them as they serve — not do everything herself.
feedback.incorrect: The problem is not the strategy, her gifting, or the team's commitment. It is that she substituted her own performance for the process of building a leader-full organization. The Action Hero instinct feels productive in the short term but destroys collective capacity.
feedback.wrongReason: Students may correctly identify the Action Hero problem but misunderstand the solution. Empowerment is not about the leader stepping back entirely — it is the specific four-step process of identifying, equipping, mobilizing, and supporting others so that the whole body functions interdependently.

variants.stem: A youth pastor personally runs every program, leads every Bible study, and handles all parent communication. Volunteers feel sidelined and eventually stop showing up. He complains that he 'can't find reliable people.' From the Week 7 framework, what is happening?
options: The Action Hero problem — his individual performance instinct is preventing him from empowering others through the identify-equip-mobilize-support process
A staffing problem — the church needs to hire additional paid youth workers
A generational issue — young volunteers today are less committed than previous generations
A vision problem — he hasn't communicated the ministry's mission compellingly enough
justifications: By doing everything himself, the youth pastor has created a system where volunteers have no meaningful role; the empowerment process requires intentionally identifying gifts, equipping people, giving them real responsibility, and supporting them as they serve
The Action Hero problem means the leader is doing too many tasks and simply needs better time management to delegate efficiently
Interdependence requires that the senior leader never performs direct ministry but only supervises others who do the actual work
The volunteers left because they disagreed with the pastor's theology, not because of how he structured their involvement

feedback.correct: The youth pastor's complaint that he 'can't find reliable people' is itself evidence of the Action Hero problem. His behavior created a system where volunteering is pointless. The empowerment process would identify their gifts, equip them, give them genuine responsibility, and support their growth.
feedback.incorrect: This is not primarily a staffing, generational, or vision problem. The pastor's own Action Hero behavior is producing the volunteer disengagement he complains about — a pattern very similar to the collusion dynamic studied in the current session.
feedback.wrongReason: Some students recognize the Action Hero trap but think the solution is simple delegation or time management. True empowerment involves the full four-step process: identify gifts, equip with skills and confidence, mobilize into real responsibility, and provide ongoing support.

questions.stem: Classify each statement about the Inward Mindset, the Outward Mindset, and the process of getting 'out of the box':

statements.text: A leader operating from an Outward Mindset will never experience frustration with a team member.
Self-betrayal requires a conscious, deliberate decision to harm another person.
A person can be 'in the box' toward one person while simultaneously being 'out of the box' toward another.
Improving organizational structures (clearer roles, better processes) will resolve conflicts rooted in an Inward Mindset.
The collusion pattern requires both parties to be 'in the box' toward each other.
A leader who has gotten 'out of the box' may still need to hold others accountable or make difficult decisions.
Using the SAM process (See Others, Adjust Efforts, Measure Impact) guarantees the other person will also get out of the box.

feedback.correct: The key distinctions here test whether you understand the boundaries of the framework. The Outward Mindset does not eliminate frustration — it changes how you hold frustration (seeing the other person as human). Self-betrayal is not intentional malice but a subtle turning away from what you know you should do. And getting out of the box is unilateral — you can change your own way of being even if the other person does not.
feedback.incorrect: Common errors include thinking the Outward Mindset means never having negative emotions (it doesn't — it means holding those emotions while still seeing the other as a person), that self-betrayal must be intentional (it is often subtle and unconscious), or that structural fixes alone can resolve mindset problems (structures help, but they cannot change what is happening at the mental-model level of the iceberg).

variants.stem: Classify each statement about self-betrayal, collusion, and the SAM process:

statements.text: Horribilizing — making another person worse than they really are — occurs only after self-betrayal, not before.
In a collusion pattern, one person is always more 'in the box' than the other and bears primary responsibility.
A leader can sincerely complete all three SAM steps and still find that the relationship does not improve.
Seeing someone as a 'vehicle' (useful for my goals) counts as an Outward Mindset because you value what they contribute.
The 'Measure Impact' step of SAM requires the leader to evaluate whether their adjusted efforts actually helped the other person.
A person who recognizes they are 'in the box' can get out of the box simply by trying harder to behave correctly toward the other person.
Blaming the other person for the relationship problem is a reliable indicator that you are 'in the box.'

feedback.correct: These distinctions reveal the depth of the framework. Horribilizing is always a post-betrayal phenomenon (it justifies the betrayal).
